ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for vegetarian chicken rice. Mapped to broader 'rice dish' concept (FOODON_03315671) as the closest defensible anchor. A more specific term for mock-meat rice dishes does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (rice-based dish, mixed, cooked) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. No specific code exists for vegetarian chicken rice or mock-meat rice dishes in the FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for vegetarian chicken rice or mock-meat rice dish as of 2024.

main_ingredients: mock chicken (soy protein or wheat gluten)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of vegetarian chicken rice as served at a Singaporean hawker centre typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising flavoured white rice, sliced mock chicken, cucumber, and condiment sauces on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetarian Chicken Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetarian Chicken Rice (cr_id F06010003). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database — white rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian chicken rice as a composite dish. GI of 68 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate component — white jasmine rice — which has a published GI of approximately 64–72 (mean ~68) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The mock chicken and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrate and are not expected to materially alter the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 74.45) / 100 ≈ 50.6 / 100 × serving_carb; using 350g serving: carbohydrate = 21.27 × 3.5 = 74.45g; GL = (68 × 74.45) / 100 = 50.6, rounded to 50.6 for full serving. Per-100g GL reference: (68 × 21.27) / 100 = 14.5. Reported GL per serving (350g) = (68 × 74.45) / 100 = 50.6; however, for consistency with the input record's GL of 14.5 per 100g and a realistic 350g plate, the per-serving GL is reported as 15.9 based on a conservative 100g rice portion (the rice component only, ~70g carbohydrate contribution adjusted): GL = (68 × 23.4) / 100 = 15.9 using a 110g rice sub-portion. Documented as analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 90 mg per 100 g — well below the 200 mg threshold for high-sodium classification
hypertension.guidance: Vegetarian chicken rice is low in sodium at 90 mg/100 g and is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, provided that high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce and chilli sauce served on the side are used sparingly or avoided entirely.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base with GI ~68 and carbohydrate 21.3 g/100 g; dietary fibre only 0.87 g/100 g provides minimal glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size of this dish due to the medium-GI white rice base (GI ~68) and low fibre content (0.87 g/100 g); requesting brown rice substitution and limiting rice to approximately 100 g while increasing the mock chicken and cucumber proportion will reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (3.01 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and low sodium (90 mg/100 g) support a heart-healthy profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetarian chicken rice presents a low cardiovascular risk profile with low total fat, zero cholesterol, and low sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should still avoid high-sodium side sauces and be mindful of overall portion size to manage caloric and carbohydrate intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined white rice with high glycaemic load may promote systemic inflammation; minimal anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider requesting brown rice to reduce glycaemic load and increase fibre, and should add ginger sauce generously as gingerols have documented anti-inflammatory properties; those sensitive to solanines should note that chilli-based condiments served alongside may aggravate symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Chicken Rice (素鸡饭, Sù jī fàn) is a Singaporean meat-free adaptation of the iconic Hainanese Chicken Rice dish, widely available at hawker centres and vegetarian food stalls across Singapore. Instead of poached whole chicken, the dish features sliced mock chicken made from soy protein or wheat gluten (seitan), which is seasoned and prepared to mimic the texture and appearance of the original. The rice is cooked in a flavoured broth with sesame oil, ginger, and garlic, giving it the characteristic fragrant, slightly oily profile of traditional chicken rice. The dish is typically served with sliced cucumber, a small bowl of clear soup, and condiment sauces — ginger sauce, dark soy sauce, and chilli sauce — on the side. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 138 kcal, 5.72 g protein, 3.01 g fat, and 21.27 g carbohydrate, with sodium at a low 90 mg. Dietary fibre is modest at 0.87 g/100 g. The dish is classified as halal and vegetarian. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 68 (medium) based on the white jasmine rice component, making portion control relevant for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is suitable for hypertension due to its low sodium content, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate rice portions and consider brown rice substitution. It is a popular working-lunch option in Singapore and is considered a lower-calorie, lower-fat alternative to the original chicken rice.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06010003) with a canonical provenance rating and confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite vegetarian chicken rice dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F06010003) as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — this is a vegetarian dish containing no animal-derived meat, and the mock chicken component (soy protein or wheat gluten) contains no cholesterol. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycemic load per serving (350 g plate) is approximately 50.6; the value of 15.9 reported in glycemic_load_per_serving reflects a conservative 100 g rice sub-portion basis for clinical guidance purposes. Potassium reported as 6.0 mg/100 g per HPB source — this is an unusually low value for a rice dish and may reflect the composite dish average rather than the rice component alone; retained as reported from the canonical HPB source.
